address: 9 Moo 1, Khlong Suan Phlu, Phra Nakhon Si Ayutthaya

description.en:
## Overview

Wat Yai Chai Mongkhon is one of the most beloved temples in Ayutthaya, distinguished by its massive 60-meter-tall bell-shaped chedi that dominates the southeastern skyline. Unlike most Ayutthaya ruins, this is a fully active Buddhist monastery where saffron-robed monks still live and worship daily. The combination of dramatic ancient ruins, a climbable chedi, rows of saffron-draped Buddha statues, and a peaceful reclining Buddha makes this one of the most rewarding temple visits in the region.

## History

The temple was originally established in 1357 by King U-Thong, the founder of Ayutthaya, as a royal monastery called Wat Chao Phraya Thai. It served as a base for Thai monks returning from studying in Ceylon (Sri Lanka). The temple gained its current name — meaning "Great Monastery of Auspicious Victory" — after King Naresuan the Great defeated the Burmese Crown Prince in single combat on elephant-back in 1593. To celebrate this victory, King Naresuan ordered the construction of the massive chedi, intentionally built taller than a chedi erected earlier by the Burmese at Wat Phu Khao Thong. After the Burmese destroyed Ayutthaya in 1767, the temple fell into ruin until extensive restoration began in the 1950s.

## What to See

The **60-meter chedi** is the main attraction. Climb the steep eastern stairway to enter the interior, where you can peer into the excavated relic chamber that once held sacred treasures. From the top, enjoy panoramic views across the monastery grounds and surrounding countryside. At the base, walk among the **rows of Buddha statues** draped in saffron cloth — a deeply photogenic sight. Behind the chedi, find the **reclining Buddha** covered in a golden cloth. The active monastery grounds include well-maintained gardens, lotus ponds, and ordination halls where you may observe monks in their daily routines.

## Practical Info

- **Entry fee:** 20 THB for foreigners
- **Opening hours:** 08:00-17:00 daily
- **How to get there:** Located 2 km southeast of the city island; easily reached by tuk-tuk (60-80 THB) or bicycle
- **Visit duration:** 1-1.5 hours

best_time_to_visit: Early morning to watch monks on their alms rounds and enjoy cooler temperatures for climbing the chedi
tips: Climb to the top of the chedi for panoramic views — the staircase is steep, so wear comfortable shoes
The rows of saffron-draped Buddha statues make for the most striking photographs in Ayutthaya
This is an active monastery — be respectful, keep voices low, and dress modestly
